relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Miriam dies at Kadesh, and the story moves forward carrying an unspoken grief.
The community has no water and gathers in crisis.
Despair turns toward Moses with death-wish language.
The people fear that they and their animals will die in the wilderness.
They name the barrenness of the place and the absence of water.
Moses and Aaron fall before Presence.
YHWH answers the water crisis.
Moses is told to speak to the rock so the people and animals may drink.
He takes the staff from before YHWH.
Under pressure, Moses speaks harshly to the people and centers himself and Aaron in the miracle.
He strikes twice, yet abundant water still comes for all.
The leaders are held accountable for failing to sanctify YHWH before the people.
The waters remember contention and contested holiness.
Israel asks Edom, addressed as kin, for passage.
The request retells the suffering in Egypt.
Israel names Divine rescue and its present place at Edom's border.
The travelers promise to remain on the road and avoid fields, vineyards, and wells.
Edom refuses passage and warns of force.
Israel offers payment for any water used and asks again.
Edom maintains the refusal with armed strength.
Israel turns away rather than forcing entry.
The community journeys from Kadesh to Mount Hor.
YHWH speaks at Edom's border concerning Aaron's death.
Aaron will be gathered to his people and will not enter the land.
Moses leads Aaron and Eleazar up the mountain.
The priestly garments pass from Aaron to Eleazar before death.
The transition happens before the eyes of the whole congregation.
Aaron dies on the mountain, and Moses and Eleazar descend without him.
All Israel gives thirty days to mourning Aaron.

contemplative_note: Numbers 20 is crowded with grief: Miriam dies, the people thirst, Moses fails under pressure, Edom closes the road, and Aaron dies. Yet water is still given, another people's boundary is respected, leadership remains accountable, and the community pauses to mourn.
